content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1126 X Street
Sacramento 95814
322-5660
                                                               A-75-12-008

                                                        December 23, 1975


Honorable Fred W. Chel
Assemblyman, 58th District
2750 Bellflower Blvd., Suite 208
Long Beach, CA  90815

Dear Mr. Chel:

     The Commission is in receipt of your letter of December 9, 1975, asking
whether services rendered by volunteers must be valued and reported.

     The volunteer services described in your letter are not reportable.
The services rendered to your district officer or campaign committee are
not contributions regardless of whether the services are rendered by students
or professional people because the term "contribution" as defined in Government
Code Section 82015, does not include volunteer personal services. If, however,
these individuals receive compensation from another source for the time
that they spend working for your district office or campaign committee,
the value of their services should be calculated and reported. For example,
you would be the recipient of a contribution if a person "volunteered" during
working hours, but continued to receive his usual salary for these activities.

     In addition, volunteer services are not income to you unless rendered
to assist you in private affairs unrelated to campaign activities or performance
of your official responsibilities. Even assistance in private affairs is
not income as long as it is limited to the mutually advantageous services
rendered by neighbors and others, which, while extremely helpful, typically
have an inconsequential financial value.

     I hope these informal remarks will be useful. Please contact Nan Hambleton,
a member of the technical assistance staff, if you have any questions.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Natalie E. West
                                        Staff Counsel

NEW:plh

DISTRICT OFFICE
2750 NORTH LAKEWOOD BLVD., SUITE 208
LONG BEACH, CA 90815
(213) 420-2471

SACRAMENTO ADDRESS
STATE BUILDING
SUITE 2184
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814
(916) 445-0492

Assembly
California Legislature

FOR CONSIDERATION
AT 2/15
REQUEST MENTION
MEETING

FRED W. CHEL
MEMBER, CALIFORNIA LEGISLATURE
FIFTY-EIGHTH ASSEMBLY DISTRICT
(EAST LONG BEACH, LAKEWOOD, HAWAIIAN GARDENS AND SIGNAL HILL)

REPLY TO:
☒ DISTRICT OFFICE
☐ SACRAMENTO OFFICE

CHAIRMAN

JOINT COMMITTEE
ON
AGING

December 9, 1975

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Gentlemen:

In connection with the future filing of disclosure
statements under Proposition 9, both individually and
on behalf of controlled committees, I would appreciate
receiving your opinion on the following:

1) Are the services rendered by volunteers to
my district office or to the campaign committee to be
valued and reported?

a) During the course of the year, we have
a number of interns who volunteer time in our district
office, some of whom are students who receive credit from
local high schools or universities for interning in a
legislative office. These persons do research, respond
to constituent inquiries, address mail, meet with constituent
groups and generally perform district office services under
the supervision of my district office staff.

2) Do the services contributed by professional
persons to the district office or to the campaign committee
have to be valued and declared by me or controlled committees?

a) In this category, I include both the
services of an accountant, who helps us in the preparation
of the campaign committee statements, maintaining campaign
checkbook and performing general accounting services for the
campaign committee; and an attorney, who provides us with

13

Fair Political Practices Commission
Page 2
December 9, 1975

advice on the nature, scope and application of Proposition 9
and other campaign disclosure laws, as well as filing
requirements. These persons render such services from
time to time without expectation of or receiving remuneration.

In the event these volunteer services, so rendered to my
district office and the campaign committee, are to be valued
and reported, then must the donor of these services also
file any kind of disclosure statements pertaining to the
volunteer services so donated?

Your early attention to this request is appreciated.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

FRED W. CHEL
Assemblyman, 58th District

FWC:ja

Chel Assemblyman, 58th District 2750 Bellflower Blvd., Suite 208 Long Beach, CA 90815 Dear Mr. Chel: The Commission is in receipt of your letter of December 9, 1975, asking whether services rendered by volunteers must be valued and reported. The volunteer services described in your letter are not reportable. The services rendered to your district officer or campaign committee are not contributions regardless of whether the services are rendered by students or professional people because the term "contribution," as defined in Government Code Section 82015, does not include volunteer personal services. If, however, these individuals receive compensation from another source for the time that they spend working for your district office or campaign committee, the value of their services should be calculated and reported. For example, you would be the recipient of a contribution if a person "volunteered" during working hours, but continued to receive his usual salary for these activities. In addition, volunteer services are not income to you unless rendered to assist you in private affairs unrelated to campaign activities or performance of your official responsibilities. Even assistance in private affairs is not income as long as it is limited to the mutually advantageous services rendered by neighbors and others, which, while extremely helpful, typically have an inconsequential financial value. I hope these informal remarks will be useful. Please contact Nan Hambleton, a member of the technical assistance staff, if you have any questions. Sincerely, Natalie E. West Staff Counsel The letter is from Fred W. Chel, a member of the California Legislature representing the Fiftieth Assembly District (East Long Beach, Lakewood, Hawaiian Gardens, and Signal Hill). He is writing to the Fair Political Practices Commission regarding disclosure statements under Proposition 9. He seeks their opinion on whether services rendered by volunteers and professional persons to his district office or campaign committee should be valued and reported. Chel explains that during the course of the year, he has a number of interns who volunteer time in his district office, some of whom are students receiving credit for interning in a legislative office. These interns do research, respond to constituent inquiries, address mail, meet with constituent groups, and generally perform district office services under supervision. He also asks about the valuation and declaration of services contributed by professional persons to the district office or campaign committee. He includes both the services of an accountant, who helps with preparation of campaign committee statements, maintaining checkbook, and performing general accounting services; and an attorney, who provides legal services.
